segments.s291-g65: به پیروزی شاه is a prepositional phrase indicating cause or occasion ('at/by the king's victory'). خستو شدند is a past plural predicative construction: خستو (a predicative adjective 'weary/settled') + شدند (past plural of شدن 'to become/come to be'), with an implicit plural subject ('they'). Word order here places the circumstantial phrase before the predicate.
segments.s291-g66: پلنگان جنگی is a noun phrase with the adjective/qualifier جنگی following the noun (typical Classical Persian order) meaning 'warlike leopards.' چو آهو شدند is a simile: چو 'like/as' + noun آهو, followed by شدند 'they became' — together 'they became like gazelles.' The plural verb شدند agrees with پلنگان.
segments.s291-g67: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded/said'; شاه جهان is a title in apposition ('king of the world'). تا introduces a purpose/subjunctive clause here ('so that / in order that'), and سلیح is the object of that purpose ('arms').
segments.s291-g68: بیارند is the subjunctive/purpose-form (classical/poetic variant of بیاورند) 'they bring' used under تا to state the commanded action. تیغ و سنان و رمیح is a simple coordination of direct objects: 'sword and spear and javelin' (a list of weapons).
segments.s291-g69: ز ... و ز ... is a coordinated prepositional phrase 'from X and from Y' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). برگستوان is a named/lexical item here following ز and linked by و to ز رومی; کلاه is the noun 'hat' that these prepositional phrases modify (i.e., 'hats from ...').
segments.s291-g70: یکی توده کردند literally 'they made one heap' — یکی qualifies توده ('one' heap), and کردند is the past plural of کردن 'to do/make' taking the hats (implied object). نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king.' Word order places the resultant noun phrase before the verb phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s291-g71: به گرد اندرش is a circumstantial phrase 'around its circumference/staff' (به گرد = 'around'; اندر + ش = 'in/around it' with the enclitic pronoun ش = 'its'). سرخ و زرد و بنفش is a coordinated series of color adjectives ('red and yellow and purple') that describe what was placed 'around' the banner staff.
segments.s291-g72: زدند is a past plural verb 'they struck/placed/raised' (here = 'they set up' or 'they fixed'). آن سرافراز ترکان درفش is the object phrase 'that proud/triumphant banner of the Turks' (آن = 'that', سرافراز qualifying درفش, and ترکان as a genitival/attributive 'of the Turks'). The verb precedes the object in the simple past narrative style.
segments.s291-g57: هر آن کس که خواهد is a relative clause meaning “whoever wishes.” هر آن کس = “anyone / whoever” (literally “every that person”), که introduces the clause, and خواهد is the present/future sense “wishes/ wants.” نباشد + رواست is a fixed classical construction: نباشد here is a negative subjunctive/optative form of بودن (“be not / would not be”), and رواست (روا + است) is a copular assertion “it is fitting/ proper,” so together they mean roughly “it would not be fitting.”
segments.s291-g58: بدین گفته is a prepositional phrase “by this saying/statement” (بدین = به + این, “by/with this”; گفته = “statement / what was said”). افزایش آمد is a simple past narrative: افزایش (increase) + آمد (came/occurred) = “there came an increase.” نه کاست is a short negative clause: نه = “not,” کاست (past of کاستن) = “diminished / decreased,” so “not decreased.”
segments.s291-g59: The front part repeats the relative pattern: هر آن کس که خواهد = “whoever wishes.” سوی شاه خویش is a directional phrase: سوی = “toward,” شاه = “king,” and خویش + ش (here split as خوی + ش) is the reflexive/possessive “his/one's own” (خویش = one’s own/possessed). Word order places the direction phrase after the relative clause it qualifies.
segments.s291-g60: گذارد here is a jussive/optative third-person form meaning “let him place / let him go” (classical Persian often uses گذارد with an imperative/permitive sense). نگیرم is first-person singular negative present/subjunctive “I will not seize/take”; together the verb phrase expresses the speaker’s allowance plus a promise not to interfere. بر او راه پیش = literal ordering “on/upon him the way before,” i.e. “the way forward for him” — بر او marks the beneficiary/target and راه پیش is the object “the forward path.”
segments.s291-g61: ز and از are poetic variants of the preposition “from,” both used here to list causes or sources: ز کمی و بیشی = “from lack and excess” (کمی = scarcity/lack, بیشی = surplus/excess). و links the pairs; از رنج و آز = “and from hardship and greed.” This is a stacked prepositional series typical in classical poetry.
segments.s291-g62: به نیروی یزدان = “by the power of God” (به as agent/instrument “by,” نیروی = genitive of نیرو ‘strength/power’ and یزدان = God). شدم is the first-person past of شدن “I became,” and بی‌نیاز is a predicative adjective “free of need / self-sufficient.” The clause therefore states cause (by God’s power) and result (I became self-sufficient).
segments.s291-g63: چو = “when.” ترکان is the subject “the Turks.” شنیدند is the simple past third-person plural “they heard.” گفتار شاه is the object phrase “the king’s speech” (گفتار = speech/utterance; شاه = king) and follows the verb in typical Persian word order for literary narrative.
segments.s291-g64: ز سر = “from the head” (ز = from, سر = head). بر گرفتند is a compound verbal idea meaning “they took off / lifted up” (گرفتند = they took/seized; بر complements it to indicate removal from a surface). یکسر is an adverb “entirely / at once” modifying کلاه “hat.” The whole line describes the Turks removing their hats from their heads at once upon hearing the king.
segments.s291-g25: گر is the conditional 'if'. از ما functions as a genitive/oblique 'toward us' ('of/against us'); the possessive enclitic ش attaches to دل producing 'his heart'. به + اندرون is a locative phrase 'within/inside', and کین بود is the predicate 'there was hatred' (بود here = 'was' in the past/stative sense). Word order is typical poetic Persian: oblique phrase + locative + predicate.
segments.s291-g26: بریدن is a verbal-noun (gerund) 'the cutting/beheading'; سر دشمن is a genitive noun phrase 'the head of the enemy' (head before possessor as is normal), and آیین بود is a copular clause 'was the custom/practice' (بود = 'was'). The whole phrase states a customary action using the gerund + 'آیین بود'.
segments.s291-g27: ور is another conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'in that case' or 'then'. بخشایش آرد is verb phrase 'bring/grant forgiveness' (بخشایش = 'forgiveness', آرد = 'brings' / present/subjunctive poetic form), and رواست is an impersonal predicate 'it is proper/permissible'. Together: 'If in that case he grants forgiveness, it is fitting.'
segments.s291-g28: همان here means 'that very thing / exactly so' and begins the clause 'همان کرد باید' = 'one must do that very thing' (notice the slightly inverted poetic order; more prosaically: باید همان کرد). که introduces the content clause 'که او را هواست' — an older construction where او را marks the experiencer/possessor and هواست means 'it is his desire/whim'. So the clause means 'one should do exactly what he wants.'
segments.s291-g29: چو + past verb (بشنید) = 'when he heard'. گفتار ایشان is a possessive noun phrase 'their speech/words'. به درد here is an adverbial/complement meaning 'with pain' or 'to his sorrow' (their words caused him distress). The clause describes the circumstance under which the following action occurred.
segments.s291-g30: ببخشودشان is a past-tense verb 'forgave' with the attached object pronoun -شان ('them'), so 'he forgave them.' The subject شاه follows the verb (poetic inversion) and آزاد مرد is an epithet 'a noble/generous man' applied to the king. Together: 'the generous king forgave them.'
segments.s291-g31: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result 'that/so that' (here: 'that they should come'), پیش او آمدند = 'they came before him' where پیش means 'in the presence of / before'. The subject of آمدند is understood (they).
segments.s291-g32: بر آن آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'about/on that wish/request' (بر = 'on/regarding', آن آرزو = 'that desire/request'). چاره‌جو آمدند is 'they came as seekers of a remedy' — چاره‌جو is an agent noun 'one seeking a cure/solution' used predicatively before آمدند. The line says they came to seek a solution regarding that request.
segments.s291-g41: بدو دست یازم is a compact verbal phrase: بدو (= به + او) = 'to/unto him', followed by دست یازم literally 'I lay/extend a hand' or 'I seize/lay hands on'. یازم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive form (classical/poetic usage where the present/subjunctive serves for a near future/intent). که introduces the following explanatory clause ('for/that'); او یار بس = 'he is a sufficient ally' (او = 'he', یار = 'ally/companion', بس = 'enough/sufficient').
segments.s291-g42: ز گیتی نخواهیم فریادرس is a short declarative clause: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز = 'from', گیتی = 'world'); نخواهیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive/negative ('we will not/will not seek' in classical usage) built on خواه (to want/seek); فریادرس = 'a rescuer' or 'one who comes to help'. Together: 'we will not seek a rescuer from the world.'
segments.s291-g43: بدین داستان زد = 'on this tale he struck/spoke' (بدین = به + این, 'on/at this', داستان = 'tale/story'). زد here is the simple past of زدن used idiomatically 'he struck/fired off' or 'he offered (a suggestion)' — in prose it functions like 'he said' or 'he proposed'. یکی نیک رای = 'a certain (one), of good counsel' (یکی = 'a certain one', نیک = 'good', رای = 'opinion/counsel'). Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + verb + further nominal modifiers.
segments.s291-g44: که introduces the content of his proposal ('that...'). از کین = 'out of/for vengeance' (از = 'from/out of', کین = 'vengeance'); به زین اندر آورد پای is an idiomatic sequence 'put the foot into the saddle' (به...اندر آورد = 'bring/put into', زین = 'saddle', پای = 'foot') — i.e. 'mount'. The verb آورد (past/stem used in a subordinate clause) expresses bringing/placing; the infinitival notion is rendered by this finite form in poetic style.
segments.s291-g45: که here functions as a causal/complementizer introducing the clause (classical usage); این باره = 'this time/occasion' (این = 'this', باره = 'occasion/instance'); رخشنده تخت من است = 'my throne is radiant' (رخشنده = 'shining, radiant', تخت = 'throne', من است = 'is mine' / 'is my ...'). Word order: topicalized time/adverbial + predicate.
segments.s291-g46: کنون = 'now'; کار بیدار بخت من است is a nominal clause: کار = 'affair/enterprise', بیدار بخت = 'awakened/alert fortune' (an epithet: بیدار qualifies بخت), من = 'my', است = 'is'. The arrangement is poetic: 'now the affair of my awakened fortune is [active].' The verb 'است' closes the nominal sentence.
segments.s291-g47: بدین (ب + این) = 'by this / with this plan'; کینه‌گر = 'the vindictive one' (agent marked by the epithet); تخت و تاج آوریم uses آوریم (1st‑person plural present/subjunctive classical form) 'we will bring/give' and lists the objects تخت و تاج = 'throne and crown'. In classical poetry the present/subjunctive form expresses intention or futurity ('we shall/ will').
segments.s291-g48: وگر = 'and if / or if' (introducing the alternative); رسم تابوت ساج آوریم = 'we will bring the custom/rite of a saj coffin' (رسم = 'custom/rite', تابوت = 'coffin', ساج = name of a wood / 'saj' specifying the coffin material, here functioning adjectivally, آوریم = 'we will bring'). The clause offers an alternative outcome: either throne-and-crown or coffin-rite.
segments.s291-g73: بخوردند here is a classical/perhaps idiomatic past plural verb meaning 'they took/struck (an oath)', functioning as the main verb of the clause; سوگندهای is the plural definite object 'oaths/vows' and گران is an adjective 'weighty/grave' qualifying those oaths.
segments.s291-g74: که introduces a content clause 'that…'; تا زنده‌ایم is a temporal concessive phrase 'as long as/we remain alive' with زنده‌ایم present plural 'we are alive'; از کران تا کران is a fixed classical phrase 'from edge to edge / from one end to the other' where از marks the starting point and the second تا marks the limit 'to'.
segments.s291-g75: همه is the subject 'all/of us'; شاه را uses the object-marking را to indicate the definite person toward whom the predication applies ('the king' as the one they serve); چاکر و بنده‌ایم is the predicate: two nominal predicates joined by و, with بنده‌ایم containing the present plural copula suffix -ایم 'we are'.
segments.s291-g76: همه دل is a plural/collective subject 'all (our) hearts'; به مهر وی is a prepositional phrase 'with/for his love' (به + noun + pronominal وی); آگنده‌ایم is a stative predicate formed from the adjective/participle آگنده plus the present plural copula -ایم 'we are filled'.
segments.s291-g77: چو این کرده بودند is a subordinate temporal clause 'when they had done this' using کرده بودند (perfect/compound past: participle + auxiliary) to indicate completed action; bیدار شاه that follows is the subject of the main clause and is a nominative noun phrase 'the alert/awakened king'.
segments.s291-g78: ببخشید is the simple past 3rd-person verb 'he forgave/pardoned'; یکسر is an adverb 'utterly/at once'; همه is the direct object 'all/everyone'; بر سپاه is a classical locative/genitive-type phrase meaning 'among the host/army' (بر + noun indicating the group affected).
segments.s291-g79: ز همشان 'of/from all of them' uses the poetic preposition ز (from/of) plus همشان (classical form meaning 'all of them' or 'together'); پس آنگه is a pair of temporal adverbs 'then/afterwards'; پراگنده کرد is a causative/perfective phrase 'he caused them to be scattered / he dispersed them', with کرد as the main past verb.
segments.s291-g80: همه بومش 'all his land' (بوم + possessive -ش 'his') is the object/extent 'his whole country'; از مردم آگنده کرد is an older-colloquial use where از introduces the material/content 'with people' and آگنده کرد means 'he made (it) full' — together 'he filled his whole land with people'.
segments.s291-g33: Poetic word order: the verb نهادند (past, 3rd pl.) 'placed/laid' comes with the object سر 'head' and the prepositional phrase بر زمین 'on the ground'. همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; the first بر is the preposition 'on/upon' governing زمین.
segments.s291-g34: Two adjectival/genitive phrases describing condition: پر از + noun = 'full of ...' (پر here = 'full (of)'); خونِ دل is a genitive/coordinated phrase 'blood of the heart' (a grief metaphor); و links to دیده 'eye', followed by پر آب کین = 'full of the water (tears) of kīn (vengeance)'. Word order places the 'full of' markers before their complements.
segments.s291-g35: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/general'; سوی آسمان is the prepositional phrase 'toward the sky/heaven'; سر کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'turned (his) head' where کرد is the past verb (3rd sg). The object سر precedes the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s291-g36: A vocative phrase addressing the divine: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic inversion of ای که/که ای used to call someone, followed by a string of epithets (دادگر, داور, چاره‌گر) in apposition — 'O just one, judge, solver'. Each epithet functions as a vocative noun.
segments.s291-g37: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very'; لشکرست is a fused predicate (لشکر است) 'is an army/host'; این‌که introduces the relative clause 'that which/whose'; سر پر ز کین is a noun phrase 'heads full of kīn (vengeance)', with ز used poetically as 'of'.
segments.s291-g38: همی is an archaic/epic adverb marking continuous or iterative action ('still/indeed'); خاک جستند is a verbal phrase 'they leapt from the earth' (خاک = earth/soil, جستند = past 3rd pl. 'leapt'); ز ایران‌زمین is the source-expression 'from the land of Iran'.
segments.s291-g39: چنین 'thus' sets up the result; کردشان is the verb 'did to them' (کرد + the clitic شان 'to them'), so 'thus He did to them'; ایزد دادگر is the agent 'the just God' placed after the verb for emphasis. The construction means 'thus the just God treated them.'
segments.s291-g40: A symmetric correlative negation using نه ... و نه ... meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. رای = 'counsel/opinion/plan', دانش = 'knowledge/learning', پای = 'foot' (here figuratively 'foothold' or 'stability'), پر = 'feather/wing' (here 'wing', i.e. power to flee). The nouns are listed in parallel to show what was denied them.
segments.s291-g1: Simple nominal + past-verb sequence: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject and آمد is the past tense 'came/arrived'. به نزدیک + noun is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the vicinity of / up to' the king; نزدِیک here behaves like a locative 'vicinity'.
segments.s291-g2: Adjective precedes noun (خردمند مردی = 'a wise man'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating origin (مردی ز توران سپاه = 'a man from Turan's host'); سپاه is the noun 'host/army'. The line uses compact, slightly inverted word order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s291-g3: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). شاه را uses the archaic/poetic را with the person served (the king) rather than a possessive construction; it marks the internal object/topic. بنده و چاکریم is the predicate 'we are servants and retainers' with چاکریم in first-person plural (یم suffix).
segments.s291-g4: جز means 'except'. به فرمان او is an instrumental/agent-like phrase 'by his command' (به here = 'by'). نسپریم is the present (first-person plural) of سپردن with the negative-prefix ن (we do not / will not entrust/hand over), so the clause says they will not surrender the land except on his order.
segments.s291-g5: کس here is negative 'no one'. از خواست یزدان = 'from the will/desire of God' (خواست = divine will). نیابد is the negative-present form of یابد (lit. 'does not attain/obtain'), used here with رها ('free') as its complement; the sense is 'cannot be freed' — poetic inversion places رها after the verb.
segments.s291-g6: اگر چه شود is a concessive construction 'even if / though it happens'. در دم اژدها is a temporal/locative phrase 'in the dragon's breath' (دم = 'breath, moment'); شود is the present/subjunctive sense 'become/happen'. The whole segment gives a vivid concession ('even in the dragon's breath').
segments.s291-g7: داند که introduces an indirect clause 'knows that ...'. خود is emphatic 'ourselves/selves' and کییم is the interrogative-identifying form 'who/what are we' with the first-person plural ending -یم; together they form the indirect question 'who we ourselves are.'
segments.s291-g8: میان تنگ بسته is a compound locative/adjectival phrase meaning 'shut/closed in a narrow (tight) space' (تنگ بسته = 'narrow and shut/tightly closed'). ز بهر چییم is an archaic/poetic way to ask 'for what reason / why are we' (ز = 'because/of', بهر = 'for', چییم = 'what are we'), completing the indirect question.
segments.s291-g17: به جان‌گر is an oath phrase: به here means 'by' (an instrumental/oath marker) and جان‌گر is an epithet 'life‑giver' (God); together they form a sworn preface. دهد ... زینهار is a verbal clause where دهد (from دادن) 'gives' governs زینهار 'a warning' — i.e. 'gives a warning' — and شاهمان (شاه + ـمان) is the possessor 'our king' placed between verb and object by poetic inversion.
segments.s291-g18: ببندیم is first‑person‑plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us bind/close' (ب‑ prefix + بند root + ‑یم). پیشش is a locative 'before him / in his presence'. میان بنده‌وار is an adverbial phrase of manner: میان here ‘in/the midst’ (poetically linking to the following word) and بنده‌وار 'in a servile/humble manner' — together giving 'before him, in a humble/servile way.'
segments.s291-g19: بدین is a fused/deictic element meaning 'in/with this' (here formed from ب‑ + دین); لشکر 'army' is the noun. اندر 'among/in' introduces the locus, بسی functions as an intensifier 'much/many', and مهتر است states existence/quality: مهتر 'chief/leading man' with est 'is' gives the sense 'there are many chiefs among this army' (poetic word order places modifiers around the copula).
segments.s291-g20: کجا is an interrogative 'where' (here rhetorical: 'where is it that...'). بندگی شاه را is 'the servitude/devotion to the king' with را marking the definite/object phrase. در خور است is a fixed predicative expression 'is fitting / is worthy' (در + خور + است); note خور here is part of the expression 'در خور' meaning 'worthy of' rather than the literal senses listed for خور.
segments.s291-g21: گنهکار اوییم is a predication meaning 'we are sinners' — گنهکار 'sinful/sinner' plus an older plural copular form اوییم (poetic/archaic) expressing 'we are'. و او پادشاست joins a coordinate clause 'and he is the king/sovereign' (او 'he', پادشاست as predicate 'is king').
segments.s291-g22: از او 'from him' / 'coming from him'; هرچ آید 'whatever comes' (هرچ indefinite + آید present/subjunctive 'comes/happens'); به ما 'to us'; بر رواست is a predicate meaning 'is permissible/fitting upon (us)' — بر is the relational preposition 'upon/against' and رواست 'is permissible/fit'. The clause means that any outcome ordered by him is allowable for them to accept.
segments.s291-g23: سران 'the leaders' (or 'heads') is the direct object. سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'one by one / head by head'. نزد شاه آوریم is a jussive/result clause 'let us bring to the king' (نزد 'in the presence of', شاه 'the king', آوریم first‑person plural jussive/optative 'let us bring').
segments.s291-g24: بسی 'much/many' qualifies پوزش 'apology/excuse' and اندر گناه 'for the sin' (اندر 'in/for', گناه 'sin/blame'). آوریم again is first‑person plural 'we will bring/offer' — the clause declares they will bring many apologies for their fault.
segments.s291-g49: وگرنه is a conditional/discourse marker 'otherwise'. به here means 'into / in the grasp of' (directional use). چنگ پلنگ is a genitive phrase 'the claw (چنگ) of the leopard (پلنگ)'; اندَرَم is the archaic locative/possessive compound (اندر + ـم) meaning 'I am in / I lie within (its grasp)' — older word order places the locative after the possessor phrase.
segments.s291-g50: This clause has the predicate-final order typical of classical Persian: مغز سرم ('the brain of my head') is the syntactic subject/affected item placed at the end, with است as the copula. خور کرگسان is an adjectival/participial phrase built from خور (present-stem 'eating') with the agent کرگسان 'vultures', producing the sense 'eaten by vultures' or 'the vultures eat'.
segments.s291-g51: کنون 'now' opens the clause. بر شما گشت is literal 'has turned upon you' (بر + شما + گشت, where گشت is perfective/become 'has become/turned'), and کردار بد 'bad conduct' is the predicate complement — word order places the change (گشت) before the description.
segments.s291-g52: شناسد here is an older-form verb meaning 'knows/recognizes' (3rd‑person singular); it is followed by the indefinite relative phrase هر آن کس که دارد خرد ('whoever has wisdom'). Note the verb precedes the noun phrase in poetic/archaic order, so the whole means 'everyone who has wit will know/recognize (this)'.
segments.s291-g53: This phrase is compact and somewhat idiomatic: نیم 'a part / not entirely' + من 'I / my' with به خون شما شسته چنگ 'a claw/hand washed in your blood'. Shifts of word order are poetic; the core idea is that the speaker refers to a hand/claw already stained by the addressees' blood (شسته = 'washed/washed in').
segments.s291-g54: که here introduces a purpose/contrast ('that / so that / as if'), and گیرم is an old-form subjunctive/optative 'I may take / I should take' (from گرفتن 'to seize, take'). چنین کار دشوار تنگ is a noun phrase 'such a narrow/difficult task' (چنین = 'such'; دشوار = 'difficult'; تنگ = 'narrow/tight'). The clause therefore expresses unwillingness to undertake that difficult, narrow task.
segments.s291-g55: همه یکسره 'all entirely' is an emphatic subject. در پناه منید is a single predicate meaning 'you are under my protection': در پناه = 'in the refuge/protection of', من is the possessor 'my', and ید is the archaic 2nd-person-plural copular ending (—ید) attached here to signal 'you (pl.) are'.
segments.s291-g56: و‌گر چند = 'and if though a few / even if there are some' (وگر = 'if / but if', چند = 'some / a few'). بدخواه 'ill-wisher' names the minority, گاه here means 'at times' or 'occasionally', and again منید carries the copular/possessive sense 'you are (under my power)'. The whole concessive clause says that occasional enemies among you do not change your place under the speaker's protection/power.
segments.s291-g9: نبدمان is an archaic/poetic negative form (contraction of نبوده‌مان / نَبودمان) meaning 'we did not have' or 'we were not (to blame)'. The prepositional phrase به کار سیاوش literally 'in/for the affair of Siavash' attaches to نبدمان to specify the domain of the negation, and گناه at the end names the quality ('sin' / 'guilt') that they lack: literally 'we had no guilt in Siavash’s affair.'
segments.s291-g10: ببرد is a 3rd-person singular verbal form (the b- prefix marking the imperfective/subjunctive/continuous aspect in classical Persian) used here narratively as 'takes/carries away' or 'led away.' The agent (اهرمن) follows the verb (poetic word order), شاه را marks the possessor with the object marker (the heart belongs to the king), دل is the direct object 'heart', and ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the path' — together meaning 'Ahriman led the king’s heart off the right path' (i.e., led him astray).
segments.s291-g11: که introduces the clause (here roughly 'for' / 'that'). توران ز ایران places Turan and the reason phrase ('because of Iran' with poetic ز), so the cause precedes 'full of grief'. همه modifies پر غم ('entirely/full of sorrow'), and است is the copula 'is'. The whole means 'Turan, because of Iran, is entirely full of grief.'
segments.s291-g12: زن و کودک خرد is a coordinated subject: 'women and small children.' Here خرد functions as an adjective after کودک (classical/poetic noun–adjective order) meaning 'small' or 'little.' در ماتم است is a locative/state phrase with the copula 'is' meaning 'are in mourning.'
segments.s291-g13: نه is the clause negator ('not'). بر آرزو is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'for the sake of desire' or 'out of a wish.' کینه‌خواه is a compound-like construction where کینه ('hatred/vengeance') combines with خواه ('seeking/wishing') to characterize the coming as motivated by vengeance; آمدیم is the 1st-person plural past 'we came.' Altogether: 'We did not come out of a wish for vengeance.'
segments.s291-g14: ز بهر is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of' (z + behar). The nouns بر و بوم و گاه form a triple in apposition — 'bar and bōm and gāh' — an idiomatic list meaning homeland / dwelling / occasion or place; the final آمدیم repeats the verb 'we came' and states the purpose: 'we came for our land, our home, and our time/place.'
segments.s291-g15: از این جنگ is a source phrase 'from this war.' ما را with را marks the affected party ('us' in a dative/experiential sense). بد آمد به سر is an idiom: آمد (past 'came') with بد and the locative 'به سر' means 'bad befell (someone)' or 'misfortune came upon us.' The verb آمد is 3rd-person past used impersonally with the affected object marked by را.
segments.s291-g16: پسر بی‌پدر شد is a simple clause: 'a son became fatherless' with شد as the past/perfect 'became.' The second half, پدر بی پسر, omits the verb (a common poetic ellipsis) and should be read as '(a) father (became) without a son' — the same copulative idea carried over; بی/بی‌پدر means 'without / fatherless.'

sentences.s291-s33: At the king's victory they were pacified; the warlike leopards became like gazelles.
sentences.s291-s34: The king of the world commanded that they bring arms — sword, spear, and javelin.
sentences.s291-s35: They gathered hats from the Bargestavans and from the Romans and piled them into one heap near the king.
sentences.s291-s36: They wound red, yellow, and purple around its staff, and they set up that proud Turkish banner.
sentences.s291-s29: Whoever wishes — it would not be fitting otherwise; by this statement there was increase, not decrease.
sentences.s291-s30: Whoever wishes to go toward his king, let him; I will not take away the path before him.
sentences.s291-s31: From lack and surplus, and from suffering and greed — by the power of God I became self-sufficient.
sentences.s291-s32: When the Turks heard the king’s speech, they took their hats off their heads altogether.
sentences.s291-s13: If he harbored hatred for us in his heart, it was the custom to cut off the enemy's head.
sentences.s291-s14: But if in that case he shows mercy, that is fitting; one should do exactly what he desires.
sentences.s291-s15: When he heard their words with pain, the generous king forgave them.
sentences.s291-s16: He ordered that they come before him; they came seeking a remedy for that request.
sentences.s291-s21: I will stretch out my hand to him, for he is a sufficient ally; we will seek no rescuer from the world.
sentences.s291-s22: On hearing this tale a certain man of good counsel spoke up, proposing that, for vengeance, he should put his foot into the saddle (i.e. mount to take revenge).
sentences.s291-s23: For this occasion my throne is radiant; now the work of my awakened fortune is (at hand/active).
sentences.s291-s24: By this means we will give the vindictive man throne and crown; or else, we will bring the custom of a saj coffin.
sentences.s291-s37: They swore weighty oaths that, so long as we live, from edge to edge.
sentences.s291-s38: We are all the king's retainers and servants; all our hearts are filled with love for him.
sentences.s291-s39: When they had done this, the alert king at once pardoned everyone in the army.
sentences.s291-s40: Then he scattered them all, and he filled his whole land with people.
sentences.s291-s17: They all laid their heads on the ground, their hearts full of blood and their eyes filled with tears of vengeance.
sentences.s291-s18: The spahbed turned his head toward the heavens, 'O just one, judge, solver (of troubles)!'
sentences.s291-s19: That is the very host whose heads, full of vengeance, leapt from the soil of Iran.
sentences.s291-s20: Thus did the just God act toward them: neither counsel nor knowledge, neither foot nor wing.
sentences.s291-s1: A messenger came up to the king — a wise man from the Turanian host.
sentences.s291-s2: That we are the king's humble servants and retainers; we will not hand over the land except by his command.
sentences.s291-s3: No one can be freed from the will of God, even if (it) happens at the dragon's breath.
sentences.s291-s4: The sovereign knows who we ourselves are — why we are shut in a narrow space.
sentences.s291-s9: If, by the life-giver, our king issues a warning, let us close ranks before him and comport ourselves humbly like servants.
sentences.s291-s10: In this army there are many chiefs; where is it fitting to be the king's servant?
sentences.s291-s11: We are sinners and he is the sovereign; whatever comes from him to us is permissible.
sentences.s291-s12: Let us bring the leaders' heads, head by head, to the king; we will offer many apologies for our sin.
sentences.s291-s25: Otherwise I lie in the leopard’s claw, and the brain of my head is eaten by vultures.
sentences.s291-s26: Now your conduct has turned bad; anyone who has wisdom recognizes it.
sentences.s291-s27: I will not, with my hand washed in your blood, take on such a narrow, difficult task.
sentences.s291-s28: All of you are entirely under my protection, and if there are a few ill-wishers, at times you are still under my power.
sentences.s291-s5: We were not guilty in Siavash's affair; Ahriman carried the king’s heart away from the right path.
sentences.s291-s6: For Turan, because of Iran, is altogether full of sorrow; women and small children are in mourning.
sentences.s291-s7: We did not come out of a wish for vengeance; we came for the sake of our land, our home, and our time.
sentences.s291-s8: From this war misfortune befell us; a son became fatherless, a father was left without a son.
